Controlling Table Placement in R Markdown PDF Reports

Practical strategies for reliable table and figure positioning in LaTeX-rendered PDF output

r
rmarkdown
latex
reporting

I did not really know how to control where tables appeared in my R Markdown PDF reports until I stopped fighting LaTeX’s float algorithm and learnt to work with it instead.

Author

Ronald ‘Ryy’ G. Thomas

Published

July 15, 2025

The gap between what you write and where it appears in the PDF is controlled by LaTeX’s float algorithm.

1 Introduction

[YOUR CONTENT HERE – 2-3 paragraphs. Open with an “I didn’t really know…” hook about table placement frustration. Reference the Stack Overflow quote from the original post:

“I have read what feels like 100 articles and threads on the topic and tried so many things I have lost count of it all but am still stuck.”

Describe the universal frustration with LaTeX float placement in R Markdown PDFs. Transition to what this post will cover.]

1.1 Motivations

[YOUR CONTENT HERE – 4-6 bullet points. Personal and specific reasons. Examples: tables appearing pages away from the text referencing them, figures jumping to the end of the document, client reports with misplaced tables, etc.]

1.2 Objectives

  1. [YOUR CONTENT HERE – specific, measurable]
  2. [YOUR CONTENT HERE]
  3. [YOUR CONTENT HERE]
  4. [YOUR CONTENT HERE]

I am documenting my learning process here. If you spot errors or have better approaches, please let me know.

The real test is printing the PDF and seeing where everything landed.

2 Prerequisites and Setup

[YOUR CONTENT HERE – 1-2 paragraphs. What readers need: R, rmarkdown, knitr, kableExtra, a LaTeX distribution (tinytex or texlive). Show library loading.]

library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(tidyverse)

3 What is LaTeX Float Placement?

[YOUR CONTENT HERE – 1-2 paragraphs in plain language. Explain that LaTeX treats tables and figures as “floats” that it positions according to an algorithm, not necessarily where you wrote them in the source. Explain the float specifiers: h (here), t (top), b (bottom), p (page), H (exactly here). Use an analogy – e.g., LaTeX is like a newspaper layout editor that moves your photo to wherever it fits best on the page.]

4 Getting Started: The Default Behaviour

[YOUR CONTENT HERE – Show a simple R Markdown example that generates a table with kable(). Render to PDF and observe where the table actually appears. Demonstrate the default float behaviour. Follow CODE -> OUTPUT -> INTERPRETATION.]

# [YOUR CODE HERE -- simple kable example]

[YOUR INTERPRETATION HERE – describe where the table actually appeared vs where you expected it.]

Understanding the gap between source position and rendered position is the first step.

4.1 The Float Specifier Approach

[YOUR CONTENT HERE – Show how to use kableExtra’s kable_styling(latex_options = …) and the float specifier options. Demonstrate [H] with the float package, [!ht], and other combinations.]

# [YOUR CODE HERE -- kableExtra with float options]

[YOUR INTERPRETATION HERE]

4.2 The longtable Approach

[YOUR CONTENT HERE – Show how longtable avoids the float problem entirely for tables that span pages. Demonstrate with a longer table.]

# [YOUR CODE HERE -- longtable example]

8 See Also

Related posts:

  • [YOUR RELATED POST 1]
  • [YOUR RELATED POST 2]

Key resources:

  • kableExtra: Construct Complex Table with kable and Pipe Syntax – The primary table formatting package for R
  • R Markdown Cookbook: Tables – Yihui Xie’s comprehensive reference
  • LaTeX Float Placement (Overleaf) – Clear explanation of float specifiers
  • Stack Overflow: Table Placement in R Markdown – The community discussion that prompted this post
  • tinytex: A Lightweight LaTeX Distribution – Minimal LaTeX for R users

10 Let’s Connect

  • GitHub: rgt47
  • Twitter/X: @rgt47
  • LinkedIn: Ronald Glenn Thomas
  • Email: rgtlab.org/contact

I would enjoy hearing from you if:

  • You spot an error or a better approach to any of the code in this post.
  • You have suggestions for topics you would like to see covered.
  • You want to discuss R programming, data science, or reproducible research.
  • You have questions about anything in this tutorial.
  • You just want to say hello and connect.
